I'd remove the check on OpenBSD version, I think it's ok to let the command fail on older versions.
Also the loop seems useless, you can write ``return self.name in self.check_output("rcctl ls on").splitlines()``

An other issue with this implementation is that a service that not exists will always been reported as 'not is_enabled'. For instance this could occur by a typo in the service name, like ssh instead of sshd. I'd expect an error here.

Something like:

```python
@property
def is_enabled(self):
    if self.name in self.check_output('rcctl ls on').splitlines():
        return True
    if self.name in self.check_output('rcctl ls off').splitlines():
        return False
    raise RuntimeError('could not determine if {0} is enabled, does this service exist ?'.format(self.name))
```

I don't agree with removing the `create_queue` argument.  I think there are probably  projects which use this library only for sending messages, and don't use the listener component at all.  In such projects not being able to allow the launcher to create a queue could be an obstacle in development.  I'm imaging a microservice-powered ecosystem in which SQS is the primary  means of communication between services, and queues are generated on-the-fly.  Some services might only be broadcasting to particular queues, which are then listened to at some point in the future by another service.
What I'm trying to say is, allowing the launcher to create a queue actually makes asynchronous architecture base on SQS a little simpler.

The default behavior of 'warnings' is to collapse identical messages.  This test purposefully does something that it thinks should generate a message.  For the 'MXNET_TEST_COUNT=100' example above, it would not.  Thus, the test should ensure, independent of the global policy, that identical messages are not squashed.  Try the following:
```
python
Python 2.7.12 (default, Nov 20 2017, 18:23:56) 
[GCC 5.4.0 20160609] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import mxnet as mx
>>> import warnings
>>> from mxnet import gluon
>>> warnings.simplefilter('always')
>>> b = gluon.nn.Sequential()
>>> b.add(gluon.nn.Dense(20))
>>> b.hybridize()
/home/dcarter/mxnet_dev/dgx/mxnet/python/mxnet/gluon/nn/basic_layers.py:83: UserWarning: All children of this Sequential layer are HybridBlocks. Consider using HybridSequential for the best performance.
  warnings.warn('All children of this Sequential layer are HybridBlocks. Consider ' \
>>> b.hybridize()
/home/dcarter/mxnet_dev/dgx/mxnet/python/mxnet/gluon/nn/basic_layers.py:83: UserWarning: All children of this Sequential layer are HybridBlocks. Consider using HybridSequential for the best performance.
  warnings.warn('All children of this Sequential layer are HybridBlocks. Consider ' \
>>> 
```
If you start over and repeat this sequence, but leave out the 'warnings.simplefilter('always')' line, then the final 'b.hybridize()' line will output nothing.
I invite you to pursue making 'warnings.simplefilter('always')' part of all gluon usage, but independent of that, I think this test should leave this line in, since that's what the test needs to pass.

Thus I think we should make sure that  the behavior of {{ next_ds }} aligns with 
{{ next_execution_date }}.

So I think we should do either:
option 1:
If {{ next_execution_date }} is None, we set {{ next_ds }} to None.

option 2:
We make sure {{ next_execution_date }} is never None. But this will change the fact that 
{{ next_execution_date }} is `None` when `schedule_interval="@once".

We want to be able to identify when an instance is returned from dictionary and when it is created fresh.  In case,  instance comes from dictionary we prevent `__init__` from being called on it. We manage this by tagging instances.  If we'd tag the instance after it is created and not at the time of returning from dictionary,  it would always appear to be coming  from dictionary and hence `__init__` won't be applied to it.

There's a small problem with this, namely, the ``schema_path`` argument will be passed to the endpoint as kwarg, because that's what ``<path:schema_path>`` will be fetched as from the URL. Because of that, the positional argument name in the actual endpoint has to match the name of the variable in path. IMO that's an ugly assumption.

Thus to make it work I'd have to change the path expression to ``{0}/<path:path>``, given that: https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-jsonschemas/blob/master/invenio_jsonschemas/ext.py#L96

For that reason I'd keep it as it is, moreover, I'd consider:

```Python
def schemas_jsonresolver(schema_path=None):
     assert not (schema_path is None)
     from flask import current_app
     return current_app.extensions[...]
```

TL/DR: intellisense is important and we should have the docstrings on those methods

We should have docstrings on any public methods we expect command authors to use. So the public methods of command group and argument context should have docstrings to provide intellisense. A classic example is the `resource_id` method that now lives in msrestazure.tools. This originally was strongly typed, which provided some kind of intellisense. When Johan converted it to just use kwargs, it became impossible to use without pulling up code somewhere to know what you can use. A docstring was added so that the user gets intellisense. This is just as important for these methods that rely heavily on kwargs.

The reader already knows that this is happening after the repository has been synced, because step one says that the repository has been synced. Something like this would read more nicely:

> 2. Update the repository's feed URL, and sync it. This simulates a change in the contents of the source ISOs.

> I see advantage in testing on content published over http as: If I want to use pulp only over insecure connection, there's no point in using https

I agree that it's nonsensical to make content available over HTTPS and then ignore SSL verification errors. But I've heard from several people who try to use Pulp Smash and immediately get confused when tests fail due to incorrect HTTPS configurations. My solution to that is to let them disable HTTPS verification errors via the configuration file. 

> And I'm not 100% sure about this, but I think that certificate that is configured in pulp smash config file, is used only for talking to pulp's api, while for using secure feed one must correctly configure pulp itself.

Pulp Smash's configuration file cannot be used to configure SSL certificates. It only supports the "verify" option, which controls whether `pulp_smash.api.Client` verifies SSL certificates when requests are made. This class is used for all HTTP and HTTP requests. It's used when making API calls, and it's used when fetching files published by Pulp.

> If I want to use https feed, there should be other tests that verify that everything works.

It's valuable to test that both HTTP and HTTPS publishing works. So let's make a separate test case for that, rather than ignoring the "base_url" set by the user.

This code can be simplified a bit. Because these operations don't context switch:
 
- checking if a key is in a dictionary
- creating a Semaphore (or an AsyncResult)
- setting a key in a dictionary

For the thread that created the lock we know the check `if not open_channel_lock.locked():` will also be `True`, so that if can be removed.

If instead of a `Semaphore` we use an `AsyncResult`, we can wait on it and get a result, which allows the thread sending the transaction to tell the waiting threads what is the transaction hash. Because of this, the mapping `open_channel_transactions` doesn't need to have a tuple, only the `AsyncResult`.

Once the transaction polling is done, we know the check with `getChannelWith` will throw, so we don't need the `AsyncResult` anymore, this allows us to delete the key from the `open_channel_transactions` map and let python's refcount delete the `AsyncResult`
